hook: You already know that data contains information about the world — but how do you extract that information as precisely and efficiently as possible? So far, you may have used formulas like the sample mean or sample variance without asking *why* those particular formulas deserve your trust. In this unit, you'll build the rigorous framework that answers exactly that question: what makes one estimator better than another?
concept: The unifying idea is that we model data as realizations of a probability distribution with unknown parameters, then develop principled methods to estimate those parameters and evaluate how well we're doing — formally captured by the theory of *point estimation and its optimality criteria*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn how to formalize a statistical model and identify sufficient statistics — the functions of data that capture *all* the information a sample carries about an unknown parameter $\theta$.
Master the method of moments, a systematic technique for constructing estimators by matching theoretical moments like $\mu = E[X]$ to their observed sample counterparts.
Apply maximum likelihood estimation to find the parameter value $\hat{\theta}$ that makes the observed data most probable, and practice deriving MLEs for a variety of common distributions.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Properties of Estimators: Bias, Variance, and MSE
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to evaluate and compare estimators using bias, variance, and mean squared error $\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$, understanding the trade-offs each criterion reveals.
Explore how estimators behave as sample size $n \to \infty$, and gain the tools to prove consistency and describe the asymptotic distributions that justify large-sample inference.
